title: Accessible Text Formats: Fonts, Spacing, and Tools That Actually Help
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You open a textbook or a PDF assigned by your professor. The text is dense. The font is small. The lines are packed tightly together. The paragraphs stretch across the full width of the page. You try to read, but the letters seem to shift and blur. Your eyes skip lines. You lose your place repeatedly. Within minutes, you are exhausted and frustrated, and you have absorbed almost nothing. The problem is not your brain. The problem is the visual presentation of the text. For neurodivergent readers, especially those with dyslexia and ADHD, the way text looks on the page significantly affects reading speed, comprehension, and stamina. The right formatting can reduce friction and make reading sustainable. Here is what actually helps and how to implement it.

Fonts Matter More Than You Think

Most academic materials are set in fonts designed for print efficiency, not readability. Times New Roman, the default for many assignments and publications, has serifs and varying stroke widths that create visual noise. For dyslexic readers, this noise makes letters harder to distinguish. The font itself becomes a barrier to comprehension.

Dyslexia-friendly fonts are designed to reduce this visual confusion. They use heavier letter bottoms to prevent flipping and swapping. They increase the distinction between similar letter shapes, such as lowercase b and d, or p and q. They space letters and words more generously. OpenDyslexic is a free, widely available font designed specifically for dyslexic readers. Lexend is a font family developed based on reading research that allows you to adjust the width and spacing. Both are available for download and can be used in word processors, e-readers, and some browser extensions.

Even within standard fonts, some choices are better than others. Sans-serif fonts like Arial, Calibri, and Verdana are generally easier to read than serif fonts. They have cleaner lines and fewer visual distractions. Comic Sans, despite its reputation as an unprofessional font, is one of the most readable fonts for many dyslexic readers because of its irregular letter shapes and generous spacing. If you are writing for yourself, use whatever font helps you read comfortably. You can change the font to something more conventional before submitting.

Spacing and Layout Adjustments

The density of text on the page affects how easily the brain processes it. When lines are too close together, they visually merge. When paragraphs are too wide, the eyes struggle to track from the end of one line to the beginning of the next. When the text is justified and creates uneven spacing between words, the irregular gaps create visual rivers that distract the eye.

Adjust these settings on any digital text you read. Increase the line spacing to at least 1.5. Wider line spacing reduces visual crowding and makes it easier for your eyes to stay on the correct line. Reduce the column width. Aim for lines that contain roughly ten to twelve words. Narrower columns reduce the distance your eyes must travel when moving from the end of one line to the start of the next, which decreases tracking errors. Align text to the left rather than justifying it. Ragged right margins create consistent spacing between words, which is easier for the dyslexic brain to process.

Many e-readers and reading apps allow you to customize these settings. Kindle, Apple Books, and Google Play Books all let you adjust font, font size, line spacing, and margins. Browser extensions like Reader View in Firefox or Reading Mode in Chrome strip away formatting and let you read articles in a clean, customizable layout. If you are reading a PDF that cannot be adjusted natively, convert it to a format that can. NaturalReader and similar tools can extract text from PDFs and display it in a customizable reading environment.

Color and Contrast

Pure black text on a pure white background creates high contrast that can cause visual stress for some readers. The white background reflects light intensely, and the high contrast can make letters appear to move or blur. This is not an eye problem. It is a perceptual processing issue that affects many people with dyslexia and ADHD.

Reducing the contrast can help. Change the background to a soft pastel color: light yellow, pale blue, or cream. Change the text to dark gray rather than black. The goal is not a dramatic color shift but a subtle reduction in the intensity of the contrast. Many e-readers and reading apps have built-in color themes that do this automatically. On your computer, you can use accessibility settings to reduce contrast or apply color filters.

Some readers benefit from colored overlays on physical pages. A transparent colored sheet placed over a textbook page can reduce visual stress. Colored overlays are inexpensive and available from educational supply companies. The specific color that helps varies from person to person. Experiment with different colors to find what works for you. If you find a color that helps, you can replicate it digitally by changing your screen background or using a reading app with color themes.

Tools That Automate Formatting

You do not need to manually reformat every document you read. Several tools can do it for you. BeeLine Reader adds color gradients to text that guide your eyes from the end of one line to the beginning of the next. The color transition reduces line-skipping and helps maintain reading flow. Helperbird is a browser extension that bundles multiple accessibility features: dyslexia-friendly fonts, color overlays, rulers, text-to-speech, and spacing adjustments. It works on websites, Google Docs, and PDFs. Immersive Reader, built into Microsoft products and available as a browser extension, lets you adjust font, spacing, column width, and line focus while also providing text-to-speech.

These tools are not cheating. They are accommodations that make text accessible to a brain that processes visual information differently. Use them. They exist because students like you need them.

The Bottom Line

The visual presentation of text significantly affects reading ability for neurodivergent students. Use dyslexia-friendly fonts like OpenDyslexic or Lexend, or at minimum choose sans-serif fonts. Increase line spacing and reduce column width. Adjust color contrast to reduce visual stress. Use tools like BeeLine Reader, Helperbird, and Immersive Reader to automate formatting changes. These adjustments are not cosmetic preferences. They are access tools that reduce the cognitive load of reading and make academic work sustainable.
